devanagari: श्रीशुक उवाच – । कालस्ते परमाण्वादिः द्विपरार्धावधिर्नृप । कथितो युगमानं च श्रृणु कल्पलयावपि ॥ १ ॥
english_devnagari: śrīśuka uvāca – kālaste paramāṇvādiḥ dviparārdhāvadhirnṛpa kathito yugamānaṃ ca śrṛṇu kalpalayāvapi

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation:
Śrī Śuka continued:
The divisions of Time commencing from its minutest unit (Paramāṇu) and ending with the period forming the lifespan of god Brahmā (consisting of two parārdhas) have been described to you, O King. The duration of four Yugas also has been narrated to you. Now listen to the periods of Kalpa (a span of time for the round of creation and subsistence of the world) and Laya (The period of the dissolution) of universe.)

devanagari: चतुर्युगसहस्रं तु ब्रह्मणो दिनमुच्यते । स कल्पो यत्र मनवः चतुर्दश विशांपते ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: caturyugasahasraṃ tu brahmaṇo dinamucyate sa kalpo yatra manavaḥ caturdaśa viśāṃpate
translation: The period consisting of a thousand revolutions of the Yugas (viz. Kṛta, Tretā, Dvāpara and Kali) is called one day (in the life) of god Brahmā. This period is known as a Kalpa, during which fourteen Manus successively reign over the world, O protector of men.

devanagari: तदन्ते प्रलयस्तावान् ब्राह्मी रात्रिरुदाहृता । त्रयो लोका इमे तत्र कल्पन्ते प्रलयाय हि । एष नैमित्तिकः प्रोक्तः प्रलयो यत्र विश्वसृक् । ...
english_devnagari: tadante pralayastāvān brāhmī rātrirudāhṛtā trayo lokā ime tatra kalpante pralayāya hi eṣa naimittikaḥ proktaḥ pralayo yatra viśvasṛk śete

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperative active second single], [perfect active first single], [perfect active second plural], [perfect active third single]
[vocative single from √ kṛ class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ kṛ class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ kṛ class 5 verb], [vocative single from √ kṛ class 8 verb]
[vocative single from √ kṛ class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ kṛ class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ kṛ class 5 verb], [vocative single from √ kṛ class 8 verb]

translation: This dissolution (Pralaya) is called occasional[1] (as it is caused by the occasion of the sleep of god Brahmā). During this period, Lord Nārāyaṇa, the Creator of the universe, withdraws all the universe within, himself and goes to sleep on the bed of Ananta’s (the serpent Śeṣa’s) body. And so does god Brahmā.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
As this Pralaya of three worlds takes place due to the condition of Brahmā’s sleep, it is called ‘conditional’ or ‘occasional’—naimittika—Bhāvāratha Dīpikā)

devanagari: द्विपरार्धे त्वतिक्रान्ते ब्रह्मणः परमेष्ठिनः । तदा प्रकृतयः सप्त कल्पन्ते प्रलयाय वै ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: dviparārdhe tvatikrānte brahmaṇaḥ parameṣṭhinaḥ tadā prakṛtayaḥ sapta kalpante pralayāya vai

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [present middle first single], [imperative middle first single]

translation: When the period of two Parārdha[2] years forming the span of life of the highest god Brahmā, comes to a close, the seven Prakṛtis or causal principles of the universe (viz. mahat or cosmic intelligence, ahaṃkāra the cosmic I-ness or ego and the five tan-mātrās—subtle elements) become due to be dissolved (and are so withdrawn) into Prakṛti or Primordial Matter. (Hence, it is called Prākṛtika).

devanagari: एष प्राकृतिको राजन् प्रलयो यत्र लीयते । अण्डकोषस्तु सङ्घातो विघाट उपसादिते ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: eṣa prākṛtiko rājan pralayo yatra līyate aṇḍakoṣastu saṅghāto vighāṭa upasādite

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperative active second single], [perfect active first single], [perfect active second plural], [perfect active third single]
[nominative single from √ rāj class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ rāj class 1 verb]
[present middle third single], [present passive third single]

translation: O King! this is called the dissolution pertaining to Prakṛti or primordial Matter. For in this Pralaya, the universe or Brahmāṇḍa which is the product of the causes enumerated as seven Prakṛtis above, gets finally dissolved as the occasion of the dissolution of these causes arrives.

devanagari: पर्जन्यः शतवर्षाणि भूमौ राजन्न वर्षति । तदा निरन्ने ह्यन्योन्यं भक्ष्यमाणाः क्षुधार्दिताः ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: parjanyaḥ śatavarṣāṇi bhūmau rājanna varṣati tadā niranne hyanyonyaṃ bhakṣyamāṇāḥ kṣudhārditāḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ bhakṣ ], [vocative plural from √ bhakṣ ]
[nominative plural from √ bhakṣ ], [vocative plural from √ bhakṣ ], [accusative plural from √ bhakṣ ]

translation: (At that time) the god of rains does not send any shower of rain on the earth for one hundred years. Then the earth becomes bereft of food-grains and beings, tormented by hunger, prey upon and eat one another.

devanagari: क्षयं यास्यन्ति शनकैः कालेनोपद्रुताः प्रजाः । सामुद्रं दैहिकं भौमं रसं सांवर्तको रविः ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: kṣayaṃ yāsyanti śanakaiḥ kālenopadrutāḥ prajāḥ sāmudraṃ daihikaṃ bhaumaṃ rasaṃ sāṃvartako raviḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ yā class 2 verb], [vocative plural from √ yā class 2 verb], [accusative plural from √ yā class 2 verb]

translation: Thus harassed by the Time-Spirit, the creatures gradually perish. The fierce sun ending the world (known as Sāṃvartika) with its terribly scorching rays, drinks up (evaporates) all liquid (water) that is in the seas, the humiḍs in the body and all moisture and under-ground water on the earth and does not release a drop of it.

devanagari: रश्मिभिः पिबते घोरैः सर्वं नैव विमुञ्चति । ततः संवर्तको वह्निः सङ्कर्षणमुखोत्थितः ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: raśmibhiḥ pibate ghoraiḥ sarvaṃ naiva vimuñcati tataḥ saṃvartako vahniḥ saṅkarṣaṇamukhotthitaḥ
translation: Then the cosmos-destructive fire emanating from the (thousand mouths of) Saṅkarṣaṇa (the serpent Śeṣa) raging ablaze, burns down all sub-terranean regions which have been already desolate.

devanagari: दहत्यनिलवेगोत्थः शून्यान् भूविवरानथ । उपर्यधः समन्ताच्च शिखाभिर्वह्निसूर्ययोः ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: dahatyanilavegotthaḥ śūnyān bhūvivarānatha uparyadhaḥ samantācca śikhābhirvahnisūryayoḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [genitive dual from √ sūr class 4 verb], [locative dual from √ sūr class 4 verb], [genitive dual from √ sūr class 10 verb], [locative dual from √ sūr class 10 verb]
[genitive dual from √ sūr class 4 verb], [locative dual from √ sūr class 4 verb], [genitive dual from √ sūr class 10 verb], [locative dual from √ sūr class 10 verb]
[genitive dual from √ sūr class 4 verb], [locative dual from √ sūr class 4 verb], [genitive dual from √ sūr class 10 verb], [locative dual from √ sūr class 10 verb]

translation: The egg of the universe is ablaze from above, below and on all sides by the flames of fire (issuing from below from Saṅkarṣaṇa’s mouth) and the scorching rays of the sun burns and looks like a ball of burnt cow-dung.

devanagari: दह्यमानं विभात्यण्डं दग्धगोमयपिण्डवत् । ततः प्रचण्डपवनो वर्षाणामधिकं शतम् ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: dahyamānaṃ vibhātyaṇḍaṃ dagdhagomayapiṇḍavat tataḥ pracaṇḍapavano varṣāṇāmadhikaṃ śatam
translation: Tempestuous winds characteristic of the end of the universe, blow violently for a hundred years and the sky is enveloped in dust and appears smoky.

devanagari: परः सांवर्तको वाति धूम्रं खं रजसाऽवृतम् । ततो मेघकुलान्यङ्ग चित्र वर्णान्यनेकशः ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: paraḥ sāṃvartako vāti dhūmraṃ khaṃ rajasā tato meghakulānyaṅga citra varṇānyanekaśaḥ
translation: Thereupon hosts of clouds of various shades of colours, roaring with violent peals of thunder, pour down heavy showers of rains for one hundred years.

devanagari: शतं वर्षाणि वर्षन्ति नदन्ति रभसस्वनैः । तत एकोदकं विश्वं ब्रह्माण्डविवरान्तरम् ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: śataṃ varṣāṇi varṣanti nadanti rabhasasvanaiḥ tata ekodakaṃ viśvaṃ brahmāṇḍavivarāntaram

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ vṛṣ class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ vṛṣ class 1 verb], [accusative plural from √ vṛṣ class 1 verb]
[nominative plural from √ nad class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ nad class 1 verb], [accusative plural from √ nad class 1 verb]

translation: Then the universe within the cosmic egg becomes transformed into one single sheet of water. When the cosmic waters have flooded the universe and everything is merged under water, the water absorbs scent—the quality of the earth.

devanagari: तदा भूमेर्गन्धगुणं ग्रसन्त्याप उदप्लवे । ग्रस्तगन्धा तु पृथिवी प्रलयत्वाय कल्पते ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: tadā bhūmergandhaguṇaṃ grasantyāpa udaplave grastagandhā tu pṛthivī pralayatvāya kalpate

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual from √ gras class 1 verb], [nominative plural from √ gras class 1 verb], [vocative dual from √ gras class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ gras class 1 verb], [accusative dual from √ gras class 1 verb], [accusative plural from √ gras class 1 verb]
[nominative single from √ gras class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ gras class 1 verb], [instrumental single from √ gras class 1 verb]

translation: When the earth has lost its essential distinguishing quality viz. fragrance, it disintegrates and ceases to exist. The fire then absorbs the property of fluidity from the waters and they being deprived of their fluidity, cease to exist.

devanagari: अपां रसमथो तेजः ता लीयन्तेऽथ नीरसाः । ग्रसते तेजसो रूपं वायुस्तद्रहितं तदा ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: apāṃ rasamatho tejaḥ tā līyante grasate tejaso rūpaṃ vāyustadrahitaṃ tadā

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperfect active first single], [aorist active first single]

[accusative single from √ rah class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ rah class 10 verb]
[nominative single from √ rah class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ rah class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ rah class 10 verb], [accusative single from √ rah class 10 verb]

translation: The winds thereupon swallow up the form or colour which is the characteristic property of fire (Tejas). Then the ākāśa absorbs the property of the wind (viz. touch) and it ceases to exist (as it is dispersed in the sky).

devanagari: लीयते चानिले तेजो वायोः खं ग्रसते गुणम् । स वै विशति खं राजन् ततश्च नभसो गुणम् ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: līyate cānile tejo vāyoḥ khaṃ grasate guṇam sa vai viśati khaṃ rājan tataśca nabhaso guṇam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [present middle third single], [present passive third single]
[present middle first single], [imperative middle first single]
[nominative single from √ rāj class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ rāj class 1 verb]

translation: The wind enters (and is absorbed) in the ether. Bhūtādi i.e. ego of the Tāmasic type absorbs sound which is the characteristic of the sky which gets dissolved in the Bhūtādi.

devanagari: शब्दं ग्रसति भूतादिः नभस्तमनु लीयते । तैजसश्चेन्द्रियाण्यङ्ग देवान्वैकारिको गुणैः ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: śabdaṃ grasati bhūtādiḥ nabhastamanu līyate taijasaścendriyāṇyaṅga devānvaikāriko guṇaiḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [present middle third single], [present passive third single]

translation: Dear Parīkṣit! The rājasa type of ahaṃkāra absorbs all the sense-organs and Vaikārika i.e. the Sāttvic type of ego does the same in the case of their Presiding deities. The Principle called Mahat (Cosmic intelligence) swallows up the Ahaṃkāra and the attributes known as Sattva and others absorb the Mahat.

devanagari: महान् ग्रसत्यहङ्कारं गुणाः सत्त्वादयश्च तम् । ग्रसतेऽव्याकृतं राजन् गुणान् कालेन चोदितम् ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: mahān grasatyahaṅkāraṃ guṇāḥ sattvādayaśca tam grasate

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual from √ gras class 1 verb], [vocative dual from √ gras class 1 verb], [accusative dual from √ gras class 1 verb], [locative single from √ gras class 1 verb]
[nominative single from √ rāj class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ rāj class 1 verb]
[nominative single from √ cud ], [accusative single from √ cud ]

translation: The unmanifest Prakṛti, being impelled by the force of the Time-spirit, absorbs the attributes (Sattva, rajas, tamas) and establishes equilibrium in them.

devanagari: न तस्य कालावयवैः परिणामादयो गुणाः । अनाद्यनन्तमव्यक्तं नित्यं कारणमव्ययम् ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: na tasya kālāvayavaiḥ pariṇāmādayo guṇāḥ anādyanantamavyaktaṃ nityaṃ kāraṇamavyayam
translation: Pradhāna i.e. unmanifest Primordial matter is not subject to the states of modifications (such as birth, growth, decay, death) produced by the divisions of time. It is beginning less; it has no end; it is unmanifest, eternal, the cause, of all, but not subject to decay.

devanagari: न यत्र वाचो न मनो न सत्त्वं । तमो रजो वा महदादयोऽमी । न प्राणबुद्धीन्द्रियदेवता वा । न सन्निवेशः खलु लोककल्पः ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: na yatra vāco na mano na sattvaṃ tamo rajo vā mahadādayo na prāṇabuddhīndriyadevatā vā na sanniveśaḥ khalu lokakalpaḥ
translation: In that stage of Prakṛti, there is no (existence of) speech, mind, modes or attributes of Prakṛti like Sattva, rajas and tamas or principles like mahat (Cosmic intelligence). There is no scope to vital air (Prāṇa) intelligence, sense or their presiding deities. There is no structure or form which can be called “the world.”

devanagari: न स्वप्नजाग्रन्न च तत्सुषुप्तं । न खं जलं भूरनिलोऽग्निरर्कः । संसुप्तवत् शून्यवदप्रतर्क्यं । तन्मूलभूतं पदमामनन्ति ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: na svapnajāgranna ca tatsuṣuptaṃ na khaṃ jalaṃ bhūranilo saṃsuptavat śūnyavadapratarkyaṃ tanmūlabhūtaṃ padamāmananti

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ jāgṛ class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ jāgṛ class 2 verb]
[nominative single from √ jāgṛ class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ jāgṛ class 2 verb]
[nominative single from √ svap class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ svap class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ svap class 2 verb]

translation: It is a state in which, there is no dream, wakefulness or sleep. There is no sky or ether, water, earth, air, fire, or the sun. It resembles to some extent the deep sleep or vacuity itself. But it is beyond the range of thinking or imagination. The Vedas declare that (this unmanifest) state is the original root of the universe.

devanagari: लयः प्राकृतिको ह्येष पुरुषाव्यक्तयोर्यदा । शक्तयः सम्प्रलीयन्ते विवशाः कालविद्रुताः ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: layaḥ prākṛtiko hyeṣa puruṣāvyaktayoryadā śaktayaḥ sampralīyante vivaśāḥ kālavidrutāḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperative active second single], [perfect active first single], [perfect active second plural], [perfect active third single]
[present middle third plural], [present passive third plural]
[nominative plural from √ ru class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ ru class 1 verb], [nominative plural from √ ru class 2 verb], [vocative plural from √ ru class 2 verb]

[nominative plural from √ ru class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ ru class 1 verb], [accusative plural from √ ru class 1 verb], [nominative plural from √ ru class 2 verb], [vocative plural from √ ru class 2 verb], [accusative plural from √ ru class 2 verb]

translation: When the potencies of both Puruṣa and the unmanifest (Prakṛti) are impelled by Time and get inevitably and thoroughly absorbed into their substratum, that is known as the Prākṛtika Pralaya (dissolution into Prakṛti or Primordial matter)[3]
purport:
Footnote [3]:
Bhāgavata Candrikā points out that potencies (Śaktis) are the products or evolutes of Prakṛti. When Prakṛti remains as an undifferentiated and inseparable attribute of Paramātman, that is the dissolution of Prakṛti.

devanagari: बुद्धीन्द्रियार्थरूपेण ज्ञानं भाति तदाश्रयम् । दृश्यत्वाव्यतिरेकाभ्यां आद्यन्तवदवस्तु यत् ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: buddhīndriyārtharūpeṇa jñānaṃ bhāti tadāśrayam dṛśyatvāvyatirekābhyāṃ ādyantavadavastu yat

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation: [4] Knowledge[5] or consciousness is the substratum of buddhi (reason or intellect), indriyas (the senses-organs) and artha the objects of these senses). It is in the forms of these three that knowledge shines forth. But whatever has a beginning and an end is unreal because of its perceptibility and as such it is undifferentiated from its cause[6].
purport:
Footnote [4]:
From 23rd verse follows the description of the ātyantika pralaya (everlasting dissolution) which is Mokṣa—final emancipation from Saṃsāra.

Footnote [5]:
Bhāgavata Candrikā takes knowledge (Jñāna) as signifying the supreme soul (jñāna-śabdaḥ paramātma-vācī.)

Footnote [6]:
Bhāgavata Candrikā: Buddhi (reason), indrīyas (senses), etc. arc like a body and have no independent existence unless Paramātman abides in them. It is he who shines through them.
Padaratnāvalī’s explanation appears to me farfetched; “During this Pralaya the Brahman who alone supports all, shines along with Lakṣmī, liberated souls (Muktas) and he appears refulgent in the form of light and has the excellent beauty that gives delight to all senses.
Behind Padaratnāvalī, there is, however, a tradition of interpretation of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa from Madhva, The founder of Dvaitism.

devanagari: दीपश्चक्षुश्च रूपं च ज्योतिषो न पृथग्भवेत् । एवं धीः खानि मात्राश्च न स्युरन्यतमादृतात् ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: dīpaścakṣuśca rūpaṃ ca jyotiṣo na pṛthagbhavet evaṃ dhīḥ khāni mātrāśca na syuranyatamādṛtāt
translation: Light, the eye and colour or form are not different from the principle of Tejas (as they are the modifications of Tejas—light or fire). Similarly, the intellect, the senses and the sense-objects are not separate or distinct from Brahman. But Brahman is quite distinct from them (as Brahman exists even when the triad—the intellect, senses and objects—ceases to exist)[7].
purport:
Footnote [7]:
Just as it is on the principle—tejas that the three—light (as a product), the eye (the recipient of tejas) and colour the property of Tejas—depend, so the reason, senses and the objects arc created by the Lord and depend on him. Hence, they cannot exist apart from God. But he is distinct from these and is termed as jyotis.

devanagari: बुद्धेर्जागरणं स्वप्नः सुषुप्तिरिति चोच्यते । मायामात्रमिदं राजन् नानात्वं प्रत्यगात्मनि ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: buddherjāgaraṇaṃ svapnaḥ suṣuptiriti cocyate māyāmātramidaṃ rājan nānātvaṃ pratyagātmani

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ rāj class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ rāj class 1 verb]
[nominative single from √ rāj class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ rāj class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ rāj class 1 verb]

translation: It is said that the state or wakefulness, dream and sleep are the states of buddhi (intellect). The plurality (such as Viśva, Taijasa and Prājña) ascribed to the pure soul is purely Māyā (illusion).

devanagari: यथा जलधरा व्योम्नि भवन्ति न भवन्ति च । ब्रह्मणीदं तथा विश्वमवयव्युदयाप्ययात् ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: yathā jaladharā vyomni bhavanti na bhavanti ca brahmaṇīdaṃ tathā viśvamavayavyudayāpyayāt
translation: Just as clouds sometimes appear and disappear from the sky, this universe which consists of parts and has a beginning and an end appears and disappears on the substratum of Brahman.[8]
purport:
Footnote [8]:
Padaratnāvalī: just as clouds appear and disappear from the sky, and the clouds and sky arc different, so is the Brahma which creates and dissolves. The world is different from it.

devanagari: सत्यं ह्यवयवः प्रोक्तः सर्वावयविनामिह । विनार्थेन प्रतीयेरन् पटस्येवाङ्ग तन्तवः ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: satyaṃ hyavayavaḥ proktaḥ sarvāvayavināmiha vinārthena pratīyeran paṭasyevāṅga tantavaḥ
translation: Of all objects consisting of parts, only parts are said to be real (in the Upaniṣads[9]) as they form the cause, just as threads (of cloth) can be seen (to exist) even if the cloth (is unwoven and hence) not in existence.
purport:
Footnote [9]:
Vācārambhaṇaṃ vikāro nāmadheyaṃ mṛttiketyeva satyam—Chāndogya Up.6.1,4.

devanagari: यत्सामान्यविशेषाभ्यां उपलभ्येत स भ्रमः । अन्योन्यापाश्रयात् सर्वमाद्यन्तवदवस्तु यत् ॥ २८ ॥
english_devnagari: yatsāmānyaviśeṣābhyāṃ upalabhyeta sa bhramaḥ anyonyāpāśrayāt sarvamādyantavadavastu yat

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]
[nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation: [10] Whatever is perceived as a cause or an effect is an illusion as causal relation is (mutually) interdependent. Whatever has a beginning or an end is unreal.
purport:
Footnote [10]:
Bhāgavata Candrikā: The principle and its modifications are interdependent and denote the same thing in its subtle and gross state. So the distinctness of an effect or product from the general principle and its speciality in modification is nothing but misunderstanding. That which has a beginning and an end (e.g. our bodies) cannot be the Soul (anātmavastu).
Padaratnāvalī: A general principle is the aspect of a thing which remains constant throughout its existence. The aspect that is not so constant is the modification. These two are different, separable aspects. Hence that which has a beginning and an end is not an essential part of the thing.

devanagari: विकारः ख्यायमानोऽपि प्रत्यगात्मानमन्तरा । न निरूप्योऽस्त्यणुरपि स्याच्चेच्चित्सम आत्मवत् ॥ २९ ॥
english_devnagari: vikāraḥ khyāyamāno na nirūpyo

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ man class 4 verb], [nominative single from √ man class 8 verb]

translation: The effect called Prapañca (or the Phenomenal universe) though apparent or visible to us, is unexplainable at all apart from (i.e. independently of) the pure Self Pratyagātman). If it were real even to the slightest degree, it would be the same as the intelligent or conscious Self and as such identical with ātman.

devanagari: न हि सत्यस्य नानात्वमविद्वान्यदि मन्यते । नानात्वं छिद्रयोर्यद्वत् ज्योतिषोर्वातयोरिव ॥ ३० ॥
english_devnagari: na hi satyasya nānātvamavidvānyadi manyate nānātvaṃ chidrayoryadvat jyotiṣorvātayoriva

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [present middle third single], [present passive third single]
[genitive dual from √ vā class 2 verb], [locative dual from √ vā class 2 verb]
[genitive dual from √ vā class 2 verb], [locative dual from √ vā class 2 verb]
[genitive dual from √ vā class 2 verb], [locative dual from √ vā class 2 verb]

translation: Satya[11] i.e. ātman (the Self) has no diversity. It is the real existence, one without a second or difference. One who believes in difference is ignorant. The apparent diversity is due to some condition (upādhi) as between the space enclosed in a pot and the unlimited expanse of the sky (both though one, appear different due to the enclosing condition of a pot), between the Sun (in the sky) and its reflections in water or between the vital air or breath inside the body and the air outside the body (in the world over.)
purport:
Footnote [11]:
Bhāgavata Candrikā; Satya means the individual soul. He quotes Tait. Up. 2.6.1
(tat Sṛṣṭvā tadevā nuprāviśat tad anupraviśya sacca yyaccā'bhavat).
A person who thinks that there is difference in the Jīvātman of gods, men etc. is ignorant.
Padaratnāvalī: “Satya” means Brahman. It is not subject to differentiation or division
(satyasya brahmaṇo rūpāṇāṃ nānātvaṃ satyaṃ na bhavati /).

devanagari: यथा हिरण्यं बहुधा समीयते । नृभिः क्रियाभिर्व्यवहारवर्त्मसु । एवं वचोभिर्भगवानधोक्षजो । व्याख्यायते लौकिकवैदिकैर्जनैः ॥ ३१ ॥
english_devnagari: yathā hiraṇyaṃ bahudhā samīyate nṛbhiḥ kriyābhirvyavahāravartmasu evaṃ vacobhirbhagavānadhokṣajo vyākhyāyate laukikavaidikairjanaiḥ
translation: Just as gold is denoted by various names according; to the form into which it is shaped, so the supra-sensuous Lord is called by various names by egotistic persons in the language of the world and in the Vedic parlance.

devanagari: यथा घनोऽर्कप्रभवोऽर्कदर्शितो । ह्यर्कांशभूतस्य च चक्षुषस्तमः । एवं त्वहं ब्रह्मगुणस्तदीक्षितो । ब्रह्मांशकस्यात्मन आत्मबन्धनः ॥ ३२ ॥
english_devnagari: yathā ghano hyarkāṃśabhūtasya ca cakṣuṣastamaḥ evaṃ tvahaṃ brahmaguṇastadīkṣito brahmāṃśakasyātmana ātmabandhanaḥ
translation: Just as a cloud, created by the sun (by evaporating water with his rays) and exhibited (by the light of the sun), becomes darkness preventing the eye (which is also a part of the sun) from getting the view of the sun, Ego (ahaṃkāra) which is evolved out of Brahman and is lighted by Brahman works as an obstruction to Jīva or individual self (which is also a part of the Brahman) becomes a bondage preventing it from having a view of the Brahman.

devanagari: घनो यदार्कप्रभवो विदीर्यते । चक्षुः स्वरूपं रविमीक्षते तदा । यदा ह्यहङ्कार उपाधिरात्मनो । जिज्ञासया नश्यति तर्ह्यनुस्मरेत् ॥ ३३ ॥
english_devnagari: ghano yadārkaprabhavo vidīryate cakṣuḥ svarūpaṃ ravimīkṣate tadā yadā hyahaṅkāra upādhirātmano jijñāsayā naśyati tarhyanusmaret
translation: When the cloud born of the sun (by the heat of the solar rays) is dispersed, the eye is able to have a view of the sun which is its own form. The same way, when ahaṃkāra (ego which works) as the limiting condition (and obscures the realization of Brahman) is destroyed by investigation (into the real nature of the ātman (soul), it realizes that it is Brahman itself.

devanagari: यदैवमेतेन विवेकहेतिना । मायामयाहङ्करणात्मबन्धनम् । छित्त्वाच्युतात्मानुभवोऽवतिष्ठते । तमाहुरात्यन्तिकमङ्ग सम्प्लवम् ॥ ३४ ॥
english_devnagari: yadaivametena vivekahetinā māyāmayāhaṅkaraṇātmabandhanam chittvācyutātmānubhavo tamāhurātyantikamaṅga samplavam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ u class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ u class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ u class 1 verb]
[nominative plural from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative plural from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative plural from √ i class 2 verb]

translation: When the bondage of the Soul (ātman) consisting of ahaṃkāra which is an evolute of Māyā and which comes in the way of the realization of the self (ātman) is severed with the sword of spiritual knowledge, such a state of ever-lasting final emancipation is known as the eternal ātyantika) Pralaya.

devanagari: नित्यदा सर्वभूतानां ब्रह्मादीनां परन्तप । उत्पत्तिप्रलयौ एके सूक्ष्मज्ञाः सम्प्रचक्षते ॥ ३५ ॥
english_devnagari: nityadā sarvabhūtānāṃ brahmādīnāṃ parantapa utpattipralayau eke sūkṣmajñāḥ sampracakṣate
translation: [12] Some persons, having (deeper) knowledge of the subtle state of things, assert that the creation and dissolution occurs continuously in the case of all beings from god Brahmā downwards, O chastiser of enemies.
purport:
Footnote [12]:
Padaratnāvalī: God Brahmā and others experience birth and death every day. Bhāgavata Candrikā: Here Pralaya means modifications in the body.

devanagari: कालस्रोतोजवेनाशु ह्रियमाणस्य नित्यदा । परिणामिनामवस्थास्ता जन्मप्रलयहेतवः ॥ ३६ ॥
english_devnagari: kālasrotojavenāśu hriyamāṇasya nityadā pariṇāmināmavasthāstā janmapralayahetavaḥ
translation: The continuous sequence of modifications through which changing things such as bodies of beings (a stream of water or the flame of a lamp) which are rapidly being; worn away by the inexorable force of the stream of time-pass, are (known as) the eternal causes of the origination and. destruction of the bodies.

devanagari: अनाद्यन्तवतानेन कालेनेश्वरमूर्तिना । अवस्था नैव दृश्यन्ते वियति ज्योतिषामिव ॥ ३७ ॥
english_devnagari: anādyantavatānena kāleneśvaramūrtinā avasthā naiva dṛśyante viyati jyotiṣāmiva

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [accusative plural from √ van class 1 verb], [accusative plural from √ van class 8 verb]

translation: (What can be observed is only very broad stages such as childhood, youth, age). But the continuous successive states (of the body) effected by the Time-spirit which is another form of the Almighty God, and which has no beginning or end, are not distinctly perceptible every moment even as the motion (per minute) of the luminaries (the planets and stars) in their course through the sky, are beyond (our capacity of) observation.

devanagari: नित्यो नैमित्तिकश्चैव तथा प्राकृतिको लयः । आत्यन्तिकश्च कथितः कालस्य गतिरीदृशी ॥ ३८ ॥
english_devnagari: nityo naimittikaścaiva tathā prākṛtiko layaḥ ātyantikaśca kathitaḥ kālasya gatirīdṛśī
translation: Thus the four types of dissolution, viz. constant (nitya), occasional or depending on condition (naimittika), relating to Prakṛti (Prākṛtika) and everlasting or final (ātyantika) have been described to you. Such is the course of Time.

devanagari: एताः कुरुश्रेष्ठ जगद्विधातुः । नारायणस्याखिलसत्त्वधाम्नः । लीलाकथास्ते कथिताः समासतः । कार्त्स्न्येन नाजोऽप्यभिधातुमीशः ॥ ३९ ॥
english_devnagari: etāḥ kuruśreṣṭha jagadvidhātuḥ nārāyaṇasyākhilasattvadhāmnaḥ līlākathāste kathitāḥ samāsataḥ kārtsnyena nājo

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ kath class 10 verb], [vocative plural from √ kath class 10 verb]
[nominative plural from √ kath class 10 verb], [vocative plural from √ kath class 10 verb], [accusative plural from √ kath class 10 verb]

translation: O most prominent of Kurus I In this way, these stories of the sportive activities of Lord Nārāyaṇa, the creator of the universe, the abode of all beings (or the centre of all power aṅd goodness) have been narrated to you briefly. It is beyond the capacity even of the birth-less god Brahmā to narrate them in details and in their entirety (even though he is endowed with the longest span of life of two parārdha years.)

devanagari: संसारसिन्धुमतिदुस्तरमुत्तितीर्षोः । नान्यः प्लवो भगवतः पुरुषोत्तमस्य । लीलाकथारसनिषेवणमन्तरेण । पुंसो भवेद्विविधदुःखदवार्दितस्य ॥ ४० ॥
english_devnagari: saṃsārasindhumatidustaramuttitīrṣoḥ nānyaḥ plavo bhagavataḥ puruṣottamasya līlākathārasaniṣevaṇamantareṇa puṃso bhavedvividhaduḥkhadavārditasya
translation: If a man is distressed and scorched in the wild fire of various types of griefs and miseries and is desirous of crossing this ocean of saṃsāra which it is extremely difficult to cross, there is no other boat (lit. raft) other than (the act of) constantly listening to and thus drinking the nectar-like stories of the sportful activities of the glorious supreme person.

devanagari: पुराणसंहितामेताम् ऋषिर्नारायणोऽव्ययः । नारदाय पुरा प्राह कृष्णद्वैपायनाय सः ॥ ४१ ॥
english_devnagari: purāṇasaṃhitāmetām ṛṣirnārāyaṇo nāradāya purā prāha kṛṣṇadvaipāyanāya saḥ
translation: In ancient days, the immortal sage Nārāyaṇa taught this compilation of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa (through god Brahmā[13]) to Nārada who in his turn taught it to (my father) Kṛṣṇa Dvaipāyana.

devanagari: स वै मह्यं महाराज भगवान् बादरायणः । इमां भागवतीं प्रीतः संहितां वेदसम्मिताम् ॥ ४२ ॥
english_devnagari: sa vai mahyaṃ mahārāja bhagavān bādarāyaṇaḥ imāṃ bhāgavatīṃ prītaḥ saṃhitāṃ vedasammitām

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [present middle first single], [imperative middle first single]
[accusative single from √ mah class 10 verb], [accusative single from √ mah ]
[nominative single from √ mah class 10 verb], [accusative single from √ mah class 10 verb], [nominative single from √ mah ], [accusative single from √ mah ]
[nominative single from √ prī class 4 verb], [nominative single from √ prī class 9 verb]

translation: It is that worshipful sage Bādarāyaṇa who was pleased to teach me this collection (Purāṇa) describing the glories of the Supreme Lord which has the same sacred status as the Vedas.

devanagari: इमां वक्ष्यत्यसौ सूत ऋषिभ्यो नैमिषालये । दीर्घसत्रे कुरुश्रेष्ठ सम्पृष्टः शौनकादिभिः ॥ ४३ ॥
english_devnagari: imāṃ vakṣyatyasau sūta ṛṣibhyo naimiṣālaye dīrghasatre kuruśreṣṭha sampṛṣṭaḥ śaunakādibhiḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [locative single from √ vac class 2 verb], [locative single from √ vac class 3 verb]

[nominative dual from √ vac class 2 verb], [vocative dual from √ vac class 2 verb], [accusative dual from √ vac class 2 verb], [locative single from √ vac class 2 verb], [nominative dual from √ vac class 3 verb], [vocative dual from √ vac class 3 verb], [accusative dual from √ vac class 3 verb], [locative single from √ vac class 3 verb]
[nominative dual from √ vah class 1 verb], [vocative dual from √ vah class 1 verb], [accusative dual from √ vah class 1 verb], [locative single from √ vah class 1 verb]
[nominative single from √ su class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ su class 2 verb]

translation:
O best of Kurus! Sūta, when requested by sages like Śaunaka and others, will narrate this to sages during the course of a long sacrificial session in the Naimiṣāraṇya (in future).
